speaker: interviewer
text: --Fischer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have to go back to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in two barracks from me, there was a personality. The name was Joseph Schmidt. It was a tenor, a singer. And I was told by my [GERMAN] that Schmidt is over there, and he sings for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one evening, I went over there. I couldn't get in in the back, but I heard him from the front how he was singing. And I knew him from before the war-- his songs, of course. Yeah. This I-- I had to mention that. Where were we? Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mauthausen. Mauthausen. OK. First morning, we didn't go to work. They chased us around, of course. You know, counting us. Sheesh, Jews are so precious we got to count them. And next day-- it was already the third day-- we went for our appel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the floor like sardines. If somebody turned, everybody had to turn. And if you went to the toilet, it was all come back. There was no place for you anymore. It was terrible. I was used to it [INAUDIBLE] itself, but it was still terrible. OK. It was the morning, and they took us to work. We came to a place. There were 222 steps to go down a ravine. You had to go down that ravine, and pick up a stone-- a rock on the shoulder-- and walk up 222 steps. On the way, there were Germans with whips. If the stone was small, you were beaten up. I took a big stone. I knew what's going on. But on the same day, I think we took with us back to the camp about six dead. That's the famous Mauthausen steps. Anyway, as a veteran of camps, I says, no. I can't survive with that thing anymore. And I've got to do something. I don't know what. Next day, we stood for appel. And so happened I was late, and I was one from the last. And I noticed a broom in the corner. When we started walking out towards the gate, I walked away and grabbed the broom. I didn't turn around or nothing-- and started sweeping. Sweep, sweep, sweep, sweep, sweep, sweep. Sweep to the left, sweep to the right. And they locked the gates. And I was with my broom. So I swept. I swept. I swept.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's now? I cleaned up. Then I went to a-- to a shed, and I see some whitewash powder. I mixed it up in a bucket, and I painted the gutters. And all of a sudden I hear, hey, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a German officer. He called me and he says, you know behind my barrack is a lot of dirt. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Go and clean it up. And then come. I've got another place for you. I was a [GERMAN]. I created a job for myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was fine. So I cleaned up. He liked me because I cleaned good. I'm a good cleaner if I want to. And I said, if I go back to the barracks tomorrow, maybe I won't have that chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was a barrack of the people who worked administration. The cooks, the kapos-- they had their own barrack. And when I went to the German officer-- because he's supposed to tell you where to sweep again-- I seen his name was Grunstad. So I went to the barrack and there was a German kapo, a criminal. With a green triangle. [INAUDIBLE]. Kapo, whatever. And I am hearing Grunstad tell me that I have to sleep here. Chutzpah-- you've got to have chutzpah, too, you know. And I slept with them. It was good. If there was a problem-- because I always swept near the bakery, and I swept near the kitchen. Another thing I noticed over there, which is worth mentioning. One day, I was called by the same officer. He was a good guy. He was a good guy. Not bad. He never picked up his hand or whatever. He says, take another man and you have to take some dirty laundry to-- a laundromat. A laundromat. Our laundry which is out of camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took the thing. We carry it. The two guys, you know, a little carriage like. And I passed by barracks. And I noticed the women, children, and men. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I went to that laundry, laundromat, I have to wait till they clean. They are going to wash that clothing. I think it was from the Germans or whatever. And I went into that camp. And I seen Jewish faces. Hey, who are you? Well, we are Jews like you. But how come you're with the children and wives? He says, we are citizens of Chile, Brazil, Argentina, all South America. And they were not prisoners. There was an internation camps. They were internment. They didn't touch them because Germany wasn't at war with them. And then I noticed some spoke Yiddish like I do. I  said, are you really from Chile? For money, you can be from Chile, too. And it was a couple hundred of those people. They had it not bad, because they were together with their families. Well, that's-- I wanted to just mention it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Mauthausen, it was already 1944. It was about seven months. I had a good job. I became fat and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a matter of fact, one day [LAUGHS] I saw someone pass by. And I ask, who was that? [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Who was that fat pig? So my meister said, well, he's swollen from hunger. No, he's a fat one. [LAUGHS] That was so funny. I was fat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, they picked us up. Even me, even some cooks-- transport. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And put us on trains. But this time, it was trains. Normal trains with seats and everything fine. As I remember, in every-- in every wagon, you know, every compartment was two SS men. And while we were sitting-- and it was a small choo-choo train travelling up the hills. When the German came in, and he spoke German to those SS. I didn't know what he was talking about. They were Ukrainians. So we had Russians on the train. They were used to translate between the officers and those SS men. Ukranian SS men. Then I knew it's from the Vlasov army. We came to Ebensee. It was high in the Alps. A camp which they built over there. Tunnels to fabricate the rockets-- V1 and V2. There were tunnels 10 feet high. And maybe 10,000 people. All the nationalities in the world. I seen even blacks from Senegal. They were in the French army, and they brought them here. There were Russian civilians and Russian military. And there were Jews, of course. And Poles, and Ugoslavs-- the whole world. And then we have to dig those-- there was a lot of equipment for a lot of men work. You  had to push cars from the tunnels out of the tunnel and dispose of them. A lot of work was there. It was hard work. I didn't have a chance to have food either, so I lost my fat-- my baby fat, of course. It was tough for a time. And one day, I worked-- yes. I worked me a locomotive. And the meister told me to throw some coal in that-- in that furnace. So I did. And he says, well, I think I'm going to keep you here. He went to my foreman. He says, I want that Jew with me. And I was on the train. On the locomotive train. It wasn't bad because nobody chased you. I used to-- not only coal, but I used to jump out in front of that locomotive and change the railroad. If you want to go right or left, I have to change it, and jump back off the train rail. That was-- everything was fine. I worked a while over there. One thing happened to me that-- when I was changing, yeah. Sometimes you have to took the wagons in the front of the locomotive. So we pushed it. And while he was coupling, he lost some steam and burned my hand. It was terrible. He sent me back to the camp. He sent me back to the camp and the Germans were funny. Terrible but funny. They could kill you at that work. But if you have an accident at work, you are something else. They took care of you. So I went  to the doctor. He put some-- some stuff on my hand and a glove. But all my skin went off the hand. It was this hand, but you don't see it anymore. Anyways, I was in the camp for two weeks. They call it the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [GERMAN]-- I forgot already. Anyway-- [GERMAN]. You don't go to work. Still, I have to live on the piece of bread and the-- and the soup. And I knew from this, you cannot live. But still I had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] One day, I noticed that from a different barrack came some Russians. And they painted the-- the bunks with green paint. Green paint. At night whenever I came from work, they used to give us the soup. So the barber used to get two soups, the tailor used to get two soups, the shoemaker, the kapos. I stand in line, too. What do you want? I said, I paint the bunks. Oh, here is two soups. OK. It's all the chutzpah. It helped. Everything helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went back to work. I couldn't get my job back on that locomotive, so I just-- it was hard work. And it's already '45. Winter '45. It was a terrible winter this time. It was snowing and everything. And when I walked, we walked through like a metal grill. And I slipped and fell with my foot in that grill. I scraped my foot, my leg, the whole shin. Oh, well. [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back into the hospital. It was already '45. It was April. We knew-- I knew how the war is, because that guy from the locomotive used to bring me papers to read. And I used to take them to camp and give it to the German kapo. And I used to get a piece of bread for it. What kind of papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a German paper. I think it was the [GERMAN] or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I learned German in school. I learned in school German. I knew it. I'd seen the battle near the Vistula. They were already in Poland. And I see the-- that Romania was evacuated. I know it's going very bad for them. They knew, too. The Germans knew, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I went to the hospital. And I was in hospital for a while until I fixed up my leg. I tried to stay a little longer. And finally, because of a German-- a Hungarian doctor, I was OK. I was good-- more like a friend with him. There were always conversations. He says, how would you like to work in hospital? I says, I'd love to. And so I will stay in hospital. It was already April. Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. I think we're coming to the end of it. 1945, I worked in that hospital. Sad thing-- food was a problem. We used to collect food in the evening for tomorrow, and in the morning was 20 dead. So we used to split the-- our food. May 7th, the tanks came in. We knew on the day before, because the Germans was-- there was no more Germans around the perimeters. They were all the [SPEAKING GERMAN], they called them. All Germans with all them carbines. Then an American tank came in. Opened the gate, and we was liberated. I was liberated a day later. A chaplain, a rabbi came, and had a-- a prayer for us, a [HEBREW]. And-- and told us that your life will be normal. Now with-- you're going to carry this on your hands. OK. I didn't want to stay in camp. And me and two friends, we went down-- then I see that the place was a beautiful place. In the Alps-- gorgeous, a big lake. And I went to a little town called Bad Ischl. Bad Ischl was a place where King Franz Joseph used to come there. And I went to the Burgermeister, Mayor of this town. I said, you've got three ethnics from concentration camp. Give us a break, and we will stay here. He says, yeah. I'll give you-- he gave us a room in that hotel. And that-- the name of the hotel was [GERMAN] Under that gold cross. Beautiful hotel. A beautiful room. Then he gave us also cards. Food cards-- so we had food. I used to go into the shoe store, says we are from camp. We need shoes. They were scared more than good, and they gave us. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the Americans came. And I used to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found an American officer. And I told him, can I get some work? I speak a little bit English. Where can I get some work? He said, well in the kitchen. OK, I'll work in the kitchen. Not for the food, just you know-- and they gave me a uniform without the-- and I worked in the kitchen. In the kitchen, I met a soldier from Brooklyn. A Jewish boy. And I'd tell him, listen, I have mishpucha in America. I have two uncles, two aunts. And can you write a letter? I says, well, I remember their city, and I remember the street, and the number of the house. No. I remember the city and the street. And I gave him the address.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle's name was Joe Lanz, and he lived in Patterson, New Jersey. That's all I knew. He said, OK, I'll send a few letters. OK. I forgot all about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After a few months, I says, it's time to go home. Home-- if you can call it home-- and see if somebody's alive. I went back to Lodz. Alas, there was nobody there. There was only a letter for me from Patterson, New Jersey. It's my uncle who wrote a letter to anybody of the Fischer family. And then I went in contact with him. And we wrote letters. Yes, he says, we will bring you over here. And he sent me a package. I said, don't send packages. Because I receive the package, it was half taken out from it. And then one day, I had an invitation from the Polish government to go to the Army. I said no. I had enough Armies. And I takes Schmulek-- Schmulek was my friend's name. Let's go. He went to the American zone for Germany. He had a brother in Paris, and he got already a letter from him. He says, let's go to my brother to Paris. So I said, let's go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Baden-Baden to the French zone. This was 1945. I would say, November. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to the French officials, the [FRENCH] military. And he showed them the letter from his brother. And I told them, his brother's wife is my sister. He says, fine, fine. You're welcome to France, but we need a visa. A visa, I said. Well, how can I get a visa? He says, about three, four months you'll get a visa. I said, oh boy. But meanwhile, he says, here's a paper to the Red Cross. You can sleep there and eat there. All right. So we took it. We went over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a few days-- it was a little town. There was one movie. And I think we went to see the movie about 18 times. The same movie because there was nothing else to do. [LAUGHS] It was 'Maria Walewska' with Greta Garbo. [LAUGHS] I knew that film by heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, I noticed in a different barrack in the third course I see German soldiers. German soldiers? I went over to investigate. And I was told they were German or French from Alsace-Lorraine, and now they are French. So they take them to France.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one officer over there which took care of them. I noticed he goes every day with the transport to the railroad. And I don't know, I smelled a Jew. I don't know. And I approached him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked him, bonjour-- that's all I knew in French, of course. Do you speak German? He says, well, a little bit. I says, are you Jewish? He says, sure, I am Jewish. I said, listen, I got here-- I got here a problem. I have a brother-in-law and a sister in Paris, and they tell me to wait three, four months for a visa. He says, forget it. Tomorrow, you'll see me go out from camp. Come with me. You got money-- German money? I says, yes, I do have. He went to the railroad, he took the money, and bought us two tickets to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: He put us on the train with those German soldiers. When the controller came, he spoke to them in French or something, and they didn't bother us. Finally, I came out to Paris. We went out the [FRENCH], I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had the address of his brother-in-law. His name was Waldman. He lived off Rue des Amandiers. Of course, I read it at the time, Rue des Amandares. And I went to a Frenchman. Pardon, pardon, Rue des Amandares? I don't know, he says. I don't know. It was early in the morning, about 5, 6 o'clock in the morning. I don't know. Finally, I noticed an [NON-ENGLISH]. A Polish army from [NON-ENGLISH]. A uniform of Poland. I cross the street. I said, hey, are you Polish? Hello. Help me. He says, sure, I'll help you. I said, do you speak French? Sure I do. Hey listen, do you know where the address is? I don't know, but let's go down the Metro, and I'll find out. And he find out. He says, yeah. It's Metro [FRENCH]. Will you take me there? He said, of course I'll take you there. And he took us over there. He paid already for the tickets. I didn't have any French money with me. And he knocked on the door, and the concierge came out. And he told them-- he asked him about Waldman. And he showed us the second floor upstairs. And we went up there. Knocked on the door. She opened. She didn't know who we were, but she figured it out. Schmulek? And well, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came out. They took care of me. I slept in the house. I ate in the house for quite a few weeks. And then I needed papers. And I found a few-- a few people which I knew from Poland. And they were friends before the war. And a matter of fact, I had a friend from our-- from our house, which left for Paris with his mother. They divorced. His mother had brothers in Paris, and she took him. His name was Radowiski, Monek. And I knew he was a friend, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they told me, you know, we will make you papers but as a Hungarian Jew. I said, who cares? So we got working papers after I changed them. And it was time to move out from the Waldmans. We took a hotel, me and that Schmulek. And we start working.

speaker: interviewee
text: Working-- at that time, you know, all the Jews, we were traitors in Paris. And almost all the Jews died in the camps. So that Waldman, he was a presser. He says, I'll show you, and you'll be a presser. He showed me a few times, and I made money very easy. And I met Rosa's sister in the street. And she told me her mother is here. I met her mother. I met Rosette. We started to go out. I moved out. I moved to a better hotel this time, I remember. And finally, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was January 19-- oh, I should remember that date. January 19, 1947. We got married. The rabbi was my sister-in-law's father-in-law. My sister who got married before. And her father was a rabbi, so he gave us the rabbi. So we got married. And I made enough money to buy an apartment. I bought an apartment on Avenue Jean-Jaures. It was the sixth floor. There was no elevator, but we were young. It doesn't matter. And one day, we went to have a picnic out of town to a lake. And I was swimming in the water, and I see a familiar face. And I followed him. I have to find out who he is. I said, come on, Rosette, with me because maybe you will have to speak French. And I said, I can see him. I looked at him. He looked at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, do you know me? He says, no. So how come I know you? I know your father. I know your mother. I'll give you their names. Ignatz, Hannah, his brother was Natek. He recognized me. It was a Radowiski. I found him. We were very good friends then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then life was normal. I lived in France for 5 years-- 8 years. My uncle used to send letters. And then he sent me an affidavit. Sent me an affidavit in 1947. I didn't go. And I said, my dear uncle, I'm getting married. So he sent me two affidavits. I still didn't go. And then my son was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 9, 1947. We were married-- '48. What am I talking about?

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Claude. Claude. And I wrote to my uncle. I have now a son. So he sent me three certificates. I made a lot of money in Paris. I got a lot of good money, and life was beautiful. So I wasn't in such a hurry to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, I had a letter from the French ambassador. He wants to see me. We went the three of us to the embassy. And he goes, listen, I have your papers in front of me. Three times you have affidavits and you didn't show up. Listen, do you want to go or not? Otherwise, there's a million on your place. So I said, we're going. He gave us six months. So I wind up the affairs. We sold everything that we could, and we came to Patterson, New Jersey. There my uncle lived, and I met 11 cousins-- imagine. Another uncle. The aunts-- the two aunts or two sisters, which married two brothers. So two aunts were with my uncles, and two of the aunts was with my aunts. Well, the aunts died. So with the uncles-- they took me in like I was with their nephew. I had an apartment prepared. Not luxurious but it was fine, you know? And I went to work. My life was normal. I lived in Patterson, let's see. From '52-- it was '52 I came to Patterson, New Jersey. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Until '62. Ten years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother-in-law went to California and came back. He said, California is paradise. Let's go to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Speak of my daughter. My daughter was born already in the United States. My daughter, Cathy, was born in 1956 on September in Patterson, New Jersey. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- it's going back. I came here to see how it is, and I liked it. I liked it a lot. So I had a house over there. I bought already a house in Patterson. So I left Rosette to sell the house. Because meanwhile, me and my brother, we bought a business here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lumber yard, which I never knew what the heck it is. But we both-- we had a few dollars, and we bought that lumber yard. Rosette came six months later. She sold that house, and she came out six months later. We lived in an apartment. The kids were already grown up. My son went to school, and then went through high school. So was my daughter. My son went to the university. He had a scholarship for Harvard. He was an excellent A student all those years. He didn't cost me a penny. Everything was scholarships. And now-- I'm proud of him. He's a professor with the tenure for life-- this is for life. In Berkley. In Berkeley, University of California. The University of Berkeley.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is married to Annie. He married also a professor. He met her at school over there. She is from [INAUDIBLE] from Washington, DC. Her father was a lawyer. He was a lawyer to six presidents. He worked for the government. Nice man, Joe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I worked in the business for 24 years. Put away for a rainy day a few dollars, and I finally retired. And here I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two grandchildren. Abraham, or Avi, which is 14. We just had bar mitzvah a few months ago in Berkeley. It was a nice bar mitzvah. And then, he had a little one, Leah. Age of Leah which is 7 years old. Nice kids. I love my children, and I love my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sometimes ask myself. I was not cleverer than anybody else. I wasn't smarter than anybody else. I was not stronger for sure than anybody else. Luck. Right place, right time. And a little chutzpah. And that's what I applied. And that's why I survived. And I survived without much hunger. I didn't hungry-- I wasn't hungry too much. There were-- there were times when I was very hungry but not too long. I was-- I always did something not to live from the rations. If you lived for the piece of bread and soup, you had to die. You had to be a muselmann first. Like what a muselmann. And you had to die. So I always had something extra.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think the war changed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war change me? I am bitter. I am angry. I am not forgiving. That's what the war changed me. I was a happy go lucky young man. I loved music, loved-- loved books, and everything. And they made an angry man of me full of vengeance. I am still angry. I am still bitter. It's hard to forget. Or some episodes just went out from my mind. I don't remember. That's what they changed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of message would like to leave your grandchildren from your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: From mine experience. Be a mensch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Have an education. Try to be somebody. Don't be a [YIDDISH]. Be honest. Truthful. And then you'll survive happy. We finished, didn't we? So how long was I?
